title: Recall: Catching All the Positives
concept: Recall measures how many of the actual positive cases the model successfully detected, avoiding dangerous missed cases.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What does high precision tell us about a model's positive predictions?

options.text: Most of the positive predictions were actually correct (few false alarms)
The model detected every single positive case in the dataset
The model is very fast at making predictions

exercises.explain: Correct! High precision means that when the model calls something positive, it is usually right.

cards.title: The missed diagnosis
cards.body: Imagine a medical test that fails to detect a serious illness in a sick patient. In healthcare, missing a positive case (a false negative) can be life-threatening.

cards.title: What is recall?
cards.body: Recall is the proportion of actual positive cases that the model detects. It asks: Out of all the real positives in the data, how many did we catch?

cards.title: The fishing net
cards.body: Think of a fishing net. High recall means the net has tiny holes and catches every single fish in the pond, even if it also scoops up some seaweed (false alarms).

cards.title: Cancer screening
cards.body: If 10 patients in a clinic have cancer, and a test correctly identifies 8 of them but misses 2, the test's recall is 8 out of 10, or 80%.

cards.title: Myth: You should always aim for 100% recall
cards.body: It sounds ideal, but a model can get 100% recall by just guessing 'positive' for everyone. The medical test would flag every healthy patient as sick, creating panic!

cards.title: Precision vs Recall
cards.body: How optimizing for recall differs from precision:

left.text:
Avoids false alarms.
But might miss some positives.

right.text:
Catches every positive.
But might raise false alarms.

exercises.prompt: If there are 50 spam emails in your inbox, and the filter catches 40 of them, what is the filter's recall?
exercises.explain: Recall is correct positive predictions divided by total actual positives: 40 / 50 = 80%.

exercises.prompt: True or false: A model that predicts 'positive' for every single input will have 100% recall.
exercises.explain: True. If the model says everything is positive, it won't miss any actual positives, but its precision will be terrible!

exercises.sentence: Recall is calculated out of all ___ positive cases in the dataset.
exercises.explain: Recall measures how many of the real, actual positive cases were caught.

exercises.prompt: Which scenario prioritizes recall over precision?

options.text: A smoke detector that alarms at the slightest hint of heat to ensure no fire is missed, even if cooking toast triggers it
A spam filter that only blocks emails containing known virus attachments, letting some spam through
A search engine that only returns the top 3 most relevant web pages

exercises.explain: The smoke detector prioritizing recall wants to catch every fire, accepting false alarms (toast) as the price.

exercises.prompt: You are designing an airport security scanner to detect weapons. Which metric should be your primary focus?

options.text: Recall, because letting a weapon through (false negative) is catastrophic, even if it means searching innocent travelers.
Precision, because we want to avoid searching travelers to keep the lines moving.
Accuracy, because we want the percentage of correct decisions to be exactly 50%.
Recall, because we want to maximize the number of innocent travelers flagged.

exercises.explain: Exactly! Security and medical applications prioritize recall to ensure nothing dangerous is missed, even if it creates false alarms. 🎉
